speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six with Elizabeth Gorski. I asked you before about whether you and your mother had shared any stories from the war. I was wondering if you and your husband shared stories either.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. Yes. As I told you, I met my husband in Lodz. Then, we came together in Paris. We did. He had, on the 6th September, when there was the appeal for all men able to carry arms, left Warsaw with his two brothers, a sister. And they left. They went east to Lviv. Then, they brought back their-- the-- his two brothers' girlfriends. They got married. And his sister's husband, their parents, and two eldest sisters stayed in Warsaw. They were living and working near Lviv while it was under Russian rule, till 19-- till June 1941. They still corresponded with Poland. They got the news that their father had died of a heart attack in April, 1940. Stefan wanted mother to come to them to Russia. He wanted mother to be together with them. Mother was hedging. She said she wanted to put a stone for her husband. She had to wait a year for that. Stefan, in the meantime, went to the Russian authorities and was granted a permit for his mother to come to Lviv. But then before she-- she was ready to leave Warsaw, Hitler invaded Russia. And that was the end of it. They left Lviv. They were-- all that's-- all I-- I know sort of from snatches he was telling me at times. They were evacuated originally in trucks. They were under heavy shellfire. Then, they went in, evacuated by trains east, and east, and east until they landed in Uzbekistan. And that's where they spent most of the war years. There was a story Stefan told me when he left. You never knew, apparently, when those trains would stop or start moving again. When the train stopped, and he went out of the coach to get some water, and he had to pass under another train to get back. And as he was crawling under, the train started moving. And it was a very long goods train. And it kept clack, clack, clack, clack, clack above his head. And his brother was standing there screaming. Stefan, don't move! Don't move! Live let! But they never knew what was happening until the last wagon went through. And here he was untouched. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they went through very severe hungry periods. There was no food then in Russia, whatsoever. They were stealing food at times. They were attacked and chased by the Uzbeks, because they stole some honey. They were all very, very sick from hunger, from the perversion. His younger sister's husband died there from starvation. The others survived. And when the or-- war ended, the just-- I don't know how. I know-- only very vaguely heard They all came back to Poland. It wasn't easy. You-- the Russian authorities were stopping people going back. So it wasn't a simple matter. But they all came back to Warsaw. And when they've arrived-- arrived here, the group of them who had survived in Russia, there was no one-- not a single person left from my whole, deep extended family. From his parents, and siblings, and aunts, and uncles, and first cousins only, we made up at least affecting 31 persons who perished in the Holocaust. They haven't found missing person there. His older brother went straight away to a camp that was organizing people who wanted to migrate to Australia. His younger brother wanted to stay in Poland. And Stefan wanted to stay in Poland. He had been a German just before the war already. And he went back to work on that army newspaper. He was not in the army. They wanted to mobilize him. But he wasn't really keen. Then, he went to Paris. He worked-- worked on that Polish paper in Paris for a couple of years. And then-- then, we went back to Warsaw. He went back to work as a journalist. And when I finished studies, I went to work as a journalist for a while. But then, I was pregnant with my third child. So I had to stop working. And because we were trying to migrate to Australia by then-- it was after '56. The Budapest uprising, things were becoming very unpleasant in the Eastern Europe. We decided to go. I did not look for work after Margo, my youngest, was born in March '58. We left Warsaw in February 1962. We went by train through Vienna to Genoa, and then by a Dutch ship to Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orania, Dutch Orania, which was used in the English as-- it was pronounced as orange. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I thought it was my-- the holiday of my lifetime-- lifetime, that ship voyage. It's taken us almost four weeks to get here. Stefan was extremely unhappy. He didn't really want to-- he knew no English. He did-- he was over 50 then.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was terrified. How are we going to-- to live? What are we going to do here? He was very, very unhappy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed in Melbourne. Well, I must have a propensity for birthdays. We landed in Melbourne on the 24th, March '62, which was the day of Margo's first birthday. And well, his two brothers helped us. They-- they found a flat for us. One of them employed. He-- he-- employed him. He had a pressing service. And Stefan started working as a presser. And I got some odd jobs through the Jewish welfare. Then, we bought a little shop in St. Kilda in Horton Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of shop was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A milk bar, sort of milk bar mix. And we ran it for 5 and a 1/2 years. Then, we sold it for next to nothing, because the owner wouldn't extend the lease. Then, we went to-- into partnership with his two brothers in a bigger shop in their town. So for the next four years, we're traveling daily an hour each way-- until they sold the shop. And then, we both found ourselves jobs, and lived happily ever after. We've educated our children. We've managed to send them all-- we arrived as not very young people. Stefan was 51 or 52 when he came to Melbourne. I was younger. He knew no language. It wasn't all that easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take until you could speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could speak English when we came to Australia already. In the ghetto, my mother had had a girlfriend who was a teacher of English. And because those schools there were very sort of sporadic, she was quite happy to teach me English just to keep herself and be occupied. She was a very talented teacher. She started by getting me to read John Steinbeck and then Somerset Maugham. That's how I began to learn my English. I had forgotten it in the years after. But before coming here, I sort of went for a few months course of English. And I refreshed it a little so that I could communicate. On-board the ship, I was the interpreter for all the-- for the group of Polish migrants with the ship authorities. But when we arrived in Melbourne, I found out that the people here weren't speaking English. They were speaking Australian. I couldn't understand a word. [LAUGHS] I could basically express myself in a simple way, maybe. But I could. But I couldn't understand what-- what they were saying. It's taken quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children ever ask you about your experiences in the Shoah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was telling them sort of bits and pieces. Yes, yes, they thought it quite interesting. But I never had the chance. It's a long time now. I really never had the chance to take them all into one story like that. So really, I'm glad that something will be left for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you say that your experience in the Shoah has influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Basically, it taught me that you can't escape where you belong, from where you belong. That's one thing. And the other thing-- it taught me that life faces us with terrible choices. The choice my mother had to let her son go or to stop him by all means; the choice she had to stay with her mother or to go and save me; I just hope that my children or grandchildren don't have to face choices like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever ask yourself why you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did. And I think that I survived because I had such a brave, tough, and enterprising mother. There was not a thing that she would not undertake if she needed. But also, I guess because we were such an emancipated, enlightened family, that we were living, dressing, speaking, thinking, behaving like Polish people rather than Jewish. That, I guess, made it easier for us to melt in when-- when we went out of the ghetto. There is one thing that my mother had never quite forgotten or forgiven. That's that she couldn't have saved little [NON-ENGLISH] She was quite crazy about her. She kept a little broken glass marble that [NON-ENGLISH] used to play in the yard. And she kept this as a talisman, as a memory of her. We could have saved her. We-- we probably could have saved her. We could have put her into another Polish family. But her mother wouldn't let-- let go of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see that you shared determination and toughness that your mother and aunt had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I'm anywhere near as tough as they are. But my kids think that I am. When I became sick 20 years ago, when I stopped smoking-- I had been a very heavy smoker for very many years. And I just broke down then. My son told me, mother, we always thought that you were indestructible. So obviously, there must have been something for them to see that-- actually, yes, in the family, I was the strong one. I was the tough one-- yes, especially here. Not-- we didn't feel that in Poland, but here, yes. I have to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to contact anyone that had hidden you in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I would have-- I was thinking whether I should contact the nuns. But what was I to talk to them about? What was I to tell them? I didn't really want to get into that. I would have loved to contact the woman there in Czestochowa. But I didn't know or didn't remember. I never knew her name. I didn't know where to look for her, whether she was still alive. Marynia's mother must have been long dead. And there was not many others. There is one more story that I would like to tell. You asked me earlier about girls that I went to school with in the ghetto. One of them I know had survived. And she had gone to live in the States. The other girl that I loved and that was-- that was an incredibly enterprising-- a strong girl at this age. She was the same age as I, about 15-- Wanda Rechthand. There were two sisters. The older one's name was Gienia. Wanda was the younger one. Wanda organized for her parents and Gienia to flee the ghetto. She organized for them a place to hide in Warsaw. She pulled out of the ghetto her girlfriend who was at school with us, Hala Piwnic. Hala Piwnic was caught by the Gestapo. And she gave them names to Gestapo. They might have been torturing her. I don't know. But they all-- they were all caught-- all four of them. So different things happen to us. I had this little friend named Wojtek, when I was about 11. He was one of the kids in my mother's summer camps. I was in love with him at 11-- not at 12 anymore, but at 11, I was. He was gorgeous. He was fair with curly hair. He was a lovely kid. The Germans caught him and pulled his pant down-- pants down. And that was the end of it. That's-- that was all they were looking for if there was any suspicion at all as far as a man went. With women, it was a little bit easier. Actually, the thing is that sort of looking at faces in the street, it was not the Germans we feared most when we were in hiding. It was Polish scum that we feared most. The Germans did not have the knack of looking at the face and saying, that's a Jew. The Poles did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But they were the ones we feared most. On the other hand, a number of Poles saved my life. So there's both sides of the coin there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why have you decided to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids asked me. Actually, I wasn't really game to do it. I-- I thought, I don't want to go back to that. It was so long ago. It's finished. That part of the story is gone. But my girls have asked me to do it. They-- they thought that I should leave something. My granddaughter is also-- Jana, is also interested in those thing. My grandson, Daniel, not so much-- he's more of a child. But yeah, she's really interested. She often asks for bits and pieces. Well, so maybe that will give them something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message that you'd like to give your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to tell them that I hope they will never have to go through what we did, that they will live in a better world. And they'll be no need for all those experiences. They might be enriching. But they are horrific and absolutely unnecessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add? Something I've left out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would love to add one thing. This friend of my mother's, Aldona, where she was sheltered for a long time and where I spent a couple of months-- I was asked two years ago to send a statement to Yad Vashem. She's been-- her children had posthumously asked for her to be granted the title of Righteous Among the Nations of the World. And I've sent my statement and was very pleased to learn earlier this year that she did receive the title. She was really worth it. And I am glad that that thing was result in her favor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your story with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Pamela.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Gorski, who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my paternal grandmother, grandma Amelia Fiszhaut. She was a very good-looking lady and highly temperamental.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But it must have been in her very late years, judging by the look of hers-- so sometime in late '30s, I would think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photograph, Mrs. Gorski?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my maternal grandmother, grandma Rebeka Markson. It's one of our holiday places. See the boys sitting with her at the table? It must have been about '36 or '37. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my aunt's older sister, Georgiana. You can see what a strong person she is from that photo. It's a very good likeness of hers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken after the war, I couldn't say exactly. But she looks 50-ish here. So it must have been sometime in the 1940s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and I. This was taken early in the war. You can see how sad we both look, how unhappy we look, when these were not happy times for us. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, and my brother, and I. That must have been taken some-- or three years before the war or so. I can see here how good-looking my father was. He looks a little like Gary Cooper.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That whole batch of old photographs was found in my aunt's apartment in Lodz. I think that grandma had had them there. Nothing at all that we had in Warsaw had survived the war. We walked out with nothing whatsoever. And that was an old album with various photos where I retrieved those few photos of ours. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my little cousin, Janka, the one who had lost her mother on her sixth birthday. This was taken before the war. Janka, looks about three there, so it could have been '38. She was quite a lovely little girl with curly, fair hair. And we were good mates even then. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this a photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Piotr as he looked at 14, at the time when the war began. He was a very big boy then already. And he considered himself a man, not a boy. That's done in-- I don't know. Oops.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a sketch of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. It was done after the war by Mr. Lipinski. And he's done her portrait and also her mother's reconstructed from an old photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she when this was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '45 she would have been about 50. She was born in I think, '96. And that was her in London. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph, Mrs. Gorski.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Stefan and I. The photo was done for my 60th birthday, which is just 10 years ago. And it was before Stefan's first stroke. He was still a healthy man there. This is about the last I have of him as healthy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Stefan's parents, his father and mother, one of the very few photos they have saved from the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's Felicja, and father's Mojzesz Ajzenberg. His name, Gorski, was changed during the war. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me who is in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, from left to right, Stefan's oldest sister, Sonia, her daughter, Janeczka and the second sister, Adela. They all perished in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when these photographs were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's been mounted now by my son from three separate photographs. That must have been taken not long before the war. They are quite recent. But I wouldn't know exactly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the Jewish cemetery in Warsaw, 1914. It's Stefan's father's grave. It's his mother, bottom left; next to her, Janeczka and Stefan's oldest sister, Sonia. Standing at the back is his aunt, Zosia and his uncle, Abram, both mother's siblings. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Stefan's father's grave as it looks now. When Stefan and his brothers came back to Warsaw after the war, they-- based on the photo that we saw earlier-- found in the Jewish cemetery the spot of his father's grave. And they put a new stone there dedicated to his father and to the other family members who perished during the Holocaust. We've been insisting on maintenance of the grave ever since. And I can see with pleasure that it's quite well kept. This is a photo from last year only.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Tell me about this list.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a list of the members of Stefan's immediate family-- mother, father, sisters, first cousins, uncles, and aunts, who died in the Holocaust. It's taken quite an amount of effort to prepare this listing. Stefan has great difficulty in expressing himself. He's got memory lapses. And even when he remembers, he cannot very often repeat names or names of places. We've got some information from his sister, younger sister, who lives in Paris. We've got some snatches of information from Stefan. I remembered some from tales that he used to tell me or his brother and sister used to tell me. And we worked for hours on compiling this list. It's a list of the members of his family who died in Warsaw, in Otwock near Warsaw, in Lukow, in Brzesc, and in the Soviet Union during the war. There is a total of 30 or 31 there. And we've tried to do it as well as we could manage to bring the truth of 31 people. I can read it now-- oh, and one also in France, one uncle who perished in France.
